extracted_information: The web content provides a comprehensive overview of recent and anticipated changes in hemp regulations at both federal and state levels, focusing on the 2024-2025 timeframe. Key trends include the extension of the 2018 Farm Bill and a widespread move by states to enact more restrictive laws, particularly targeting intoxicating hemp-derived cannabinoids through 'total THC' limits and bans on synthetically derived compounds. It details specific proposed legislation, regulations, and already passed laws in several states, outlining new definitions and compliance requirements.

specifications.federal_hemp_definition_2018_farm_bill: Cannabis (Cannabis sativa L.) and derivatives with <= 0.3% delta-9 THC on a dry weight basis, removed from Controlled Substances Act.
specifications.total_thc_concept: Many states imposing limits based on 'total THC', which includes delta-9 THC and other potentially intoxicating cannabinoids or their acidic precursors (e.g., THCA).
specifications.synthetic_cannabinoid_prohibition: Some states moving to ban or restrict synthetically or chemically manipulated cannabinoids (e.g., delta-8 THC, delta-10 THC, HHC, THCA, THCP, THCVA) due to concerns about isomerization processes.

statistics.delta_8_thc_use_study_dec_2023: Among cannabis users, those in states without legal marijuana were >2x more likely to have used delta-8 THC (approx. 12% of participants used delta-8 THC, 25% used an 'emerging cannabinoid').

federal_level.farm_bill_status: 2018 Farm Bill extended through September 30, 2024. The 2023 Farm Bill was expected to address intoxicating hemp products but was delayed.

california.ab_2223_details_proposed: Seeks to add 'synthetically derived cannabinoid' definition (substance from chemical reaction changing molecular structure of Cannabis sativa L. extract, excluding decarboxylation from naturally occurring cannabinoid acid). Amends 'industrial hemp' definition to prohibit any synthetically derived cannabinoid.
california.ab_45_details_existing: Adopted Oct 2021. Applies 'total THC' definition (sum of THC + THCA, with THC including any THC type 'however derived'). 0.3% total THC limit. Exceeding limit makes product 'adulterated' or considered cannabis, regulated by DCC. Prohibits sale of inhalable hemp products until specific regulations developed and tax imposed (Cal. Health & Safety Code § 111929 and 111929.2).
compliance_requirements: Total THC (THC + THCA, all THC types) must not exceed 0.3% dry weight basis.
Inhalable hemp products are prohibited until specific regulations are developed and taxes are imposed.

nebraska.lb_999_details_proposed: Clarifies LB 657 (Nebraska Hemp Farming Act) did not legalize synthetic products. Aims to make CBD products with THC above legal limits, especially synthetic Delta-8 THC and similar Delta compounds, illegal. Would shift hemp cultivation regulation from Nebraska Department of Agriculture (NDA) to U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA).
compliance_requirements: Synthetic Delta-8 THC and similar Delta compounds likely to become illegal.
Hemp producers would need to apply for USDA production program licenses if LB 999 passes.

florida.details_proposed: Limit delta-9 THC to 2mg per serving or 10mg per container (whichever is less). Create 'Total delta-9-tetrahydrocannabinol concentration' defined as [delta-9 tetrahydrocannabinol] + (0.877 x [delta-9-tetrahydrocannabinolic acid]), with a 0.3% limit for legal hemp. Prohibit 'synthetic or naturally occurring versions' of controlled substances like delta-8 THC, delta-10 THC, hexahydrocannabinol (HHC), tetrahydrocannabinol acetate (THCA), tetrahydrocannabiphorol (THCP), and tetrahydrocannabivarin (THCVA). Expand restrictions on advertising and packaging.
compliance_requirements: Delta-9 THC: Max 2mg/serving OR 10mg/container (whichever is less).
Prohibition of specified synthetic or naturally occurring intoxicating cannabinoids.
Adherence to expanded advertising and packaging restrictions.

south_dakota.hb_1125_details_proposed: Seeks to ban sale of chemically modified or converted hemp-derived products (e.g., delta-8 THC, delta-10 THC). Introduces 'chemically derived cannabinoid' definition ('chemical substance created by a chemical reaction that changes the molecular structure of any chemical substance derived from the cannabis plant').
compliance_requirements: Prohibition of chemically derived cannabinoids, specifically targeting products like delta-8 THC and delta-10 THC created via isomerization.

tennessee.details_proposed: Tennessee Department of Agriculture (TDA) proposed regulations (Dec 2023) implementing a 'total THC' definition for legal hemp products. 'Total THC' defined as 'the potential total delta-9 tetrahydrocannabinol (THC) content', calculated as: (cannabinoid concentration (mg/g)) + (cannabinoid acid form concentration (mg/g) x 0.877). If adopted, likely effective after July 1, 2024. Products with >~0.34% THCA would become non-compliant/illegal.
compliance_requirements: Total THC content calculated by (cannabinoid concentration (mg/g)) + (cannabinoid acid form concentration (mg/g) x 0.877) must comply with limits (implicitly 0.3% delta-9 THC limit applied to total calculation).
Products with >~0.34% THCA likely to be non-compliant.

arkansas.act_629_details_passed: Banned production and sale of products containing delta-8 THC, delta-9 THC, and delta-10 THC and other cannabinoids. Enforcement blocked by federal court (Sep 2023), currently before Eighth Circuit.
compliance_requirements: Act 629, if enforceable, would ban delta-8 THC, delta-9 THC, and delta-10 THC products.

alaska.details_passed: Prohibits hemp products containing 'delta-9-THC or a non-naturally occurring cannabinoid, including a cannabinoid made from an ingredient extracted from industrial hemp and modified beyond its original form.' Requires 'total THC' testing, defined as 'the sum of the THC and THCA content and reported on a dry weight basis.'
compliance_requirements: Prohibition of delta-9-THC (as a non-naturally occurring cannabinoid) and any non-naturally occurring cannabinoids (modified from original form).
Hemp must be tested for 'total THC' (sum of THC and THCA content, dry weight basis).

virginia.details_passed: Hemp products may not exceed 0.3% 'total THC'. 'Total THC' is calculated as the sum of all tetrahydrocannabinol and tetrahydrocannabinolic acid, including all naturally occurring and synthetic tetrahydrocannabinols, their salts and isomers. Aggressive enforcement by VDACS with penalties ($13,000-$97,500).
compliance_requirements: Total THC must not exceed 0.3% (calculated as sum of all tetrahydrocannabinol and tetrahydrocannabinolic acid, including synthetic/natural THC/isomers).
Strict enforcement with significant penalties for non-compliance.

references.text: The 2018 Farm Bill defines hemp as “the plant Cannabis sativa L. and any part of that plant, including the seeds thereof and all derivatives, extracts, cannabinoids, isomers, acids, salts, and salts of isomers, whether growing or not, with a delta-9 [THC] concentration of not more than 0.3 percent on a dry weight basis.”
Hemp Production and the 2018 Farm Bill – 07/25/2019 | FDA
Delta-8 THC Generated $2 Billion In Revenue In Two Years, Report Finds (forbes.com)
Approximately 25% of respondents reported using an “emerging cannabinoid” in the past year, with about 12% of participants specifically indicating use of delta-8 THC.
Importantly, this new term “does not include cannabinoids produced by decarboxylation from a naturally occurring cannabinoid acid without the use of a chemical catalyst.”
